segments.text: Part 3E: Inhibition and Special mechanisms Dr. Shahir Rizk

Drugs as enzyme inhibitors
Many drugs are enzyme inhibitors
HIV-1 protease inhibitors
Penicillin: suicide inhibitor of cell-wall synthesis
Aspirin: inhibits inflammatory response
Many cancer drugs (inhibit cell division)
Drug design:
Goal is to find a potent inhibitor for a target enzyme
Potent inhibitor: can inhibit an enzyme at low concentration

Potency of an inhibitor (Ki)
The ability of a drug to elicit an effect at low concentrations
High potency = effect at low drug concentration
The inhibition constant Ki : measure of potency
Ki: concentration of Inhibitor [I] that elicits half of the maximal inhibition
Relative enzyme activity
Ki

Drug design
More potent inhibitor = lowest Ki
Low Ki = it takes very little of the drug to inhibit the enzyme
Using less of a drug = avoiding high dose, which usually means less severe side effects
[Drug]
A
B
Enzyme activity
Which of the following drugs is a more potent inhibitor?
Drug A is more potent because it has the lower Ki (curve is shifted to the left)

Example of Ki: Inhibitor of adenosine deaminase
(b) Purine riboside inhibits adenosine deaminase . The hydrated form is an analog of the transition state of the reaction. Km/Ki is measure of potency

Adenosine Deaminase
Adenosine deaminase
Enzyme that converts adenosine to inosine
Part of purine degradation (recycling DNA & RNA)
Removes excess adenosine
Relies on a catalytic Zn in the active site
Zinc is a “cofactor”

Cofactors
Cofactors (Coenzymes) are non-protein components that participate in the function of the protein
Serine and aspartic proteases DO NOT require coenzymes, because catalysis is carried out by amino acid side chains
Many enzymes rely on cofactors to expand the chemical diversity of the 20 amino acids
Coenzymes include:
Metals (Mg, Zn, Fe, Ni, Cu, Mo, W, etc )
Many vitamins are cofactors
e. g. B vitamins, vitamin C

Metal catalysis
Zn 2+ ion can allow a base ( Histidine ) to abstract a proton from water, forming OH -
OH - is a powerful nucleophile, resulting in addition of a –OH to the ring (tetrahedral intermediate forms)
Glu396 acts as a general acid, then a general base
Amino group is released, product is formed
:B
H
:

Structure of Adenosine deaminase
The structure shows the Zn ion coordinated by 3 histidine side chains
Let’s look at the structure!

What about “non-spontaneous” reactions?
While enzymes can speed up the reaction by lowering the activation energy, they are unable to change the overall ∆G of the reaction
In many cases, a non-spontaneous reaction is required, how do enzymes overcome this problem?
Solution: coupling

Example
First step in metabolizing glucose is phosphorylation
Glucose + PO 4  Glucose-6-Phosphate + H 2 O (∆G = 13.8 kJ/ mol )
Adding a phosphate group to glucose is non-spontaneous
Enzyme uses ATP as a source of PO 4
Breakdown of ATP is highly favorable
ATP + H 2 O  ADP + PO 4 (∆G = -30.5 kJ/ mol )
Coupling ATP hydrolysis with phosphorylation gives an overall spontaneous reaction

ATP as a source of energy
Adenosine triphosphate (ATP)
∆G = -30.5 kJ/ mol
ATP hydrolysis releases 30.5 kJ/ mol of energy
This energy can be harnessed by enzymes to carryout reactions with positive ∆G
Energy

Coupling
Using Hess’s law, the sum of ∆G values from two reactions = the ∆G of the net reaction
Can you write down the individual reactions and the net reaction?
Net reaction

Coupling
All that matters in the net reaction
Glucose + PO 4  glucose-6-phos. + H 2 O  (13.8 kJ/ mol )
ATP + H 2 O        ADP + PO 4 (-30.5 kJ/ mol )
______________________________________________
Net:     Glucose + ATP  glucose-6-phos. + ADP (-16.7 kJ/ mol )
The reason why this works is because ∆G is a state function
Only depends on the difference between the initial and final states

Can enzymes be designed?
Enzymes catalyze chemical reactions by stabilizing transition states
Enzymes have the highest affinity for the transition state
If we can “design” a protein that binds to a transition state at high affinity, it may carryout the desired chemical reactions
A class of designer enzymes is known as Catalytic Antibodies

Antibodies
Antibodies ( immunoglobulins )
Produced by immune cells (B-lymphocytes)
Bind to and neutralize foreign molecule (antigens)
Antibodies have very high affinity for the antigen
The antigen binding site is well suited to fit the antigen
So, what if we use a transition state analog as the antigen?

Catalytic antibodies
“Immunizing” an animal with a TS analog as a foreign antigen
Result: antibodies that have high affinity for TS
Given the substrate, some of these antibodies may catalyze the reaction by forcing the S to adopt a TS
Catalytic antibodies are often called “ Abzymes ”
Advantage: can catalyze “non-natural” reactions
Disadvantage: typically slow kinetics, high Km values, not as efficient as “natural” enzymes
Antibody antigen interactions are not nearly as high as natural enzymes’ affinity for a TS
( Kd : 10 -9 -10 -12 M vs 10 -20 -10 -26 M)

Computational enzyme design
In few examples, enzymes can be designed by computational modeling
A computer model of the TS is used to “place” catalytic groups
Computer algorithm finds best positions to place groups in a protein “scaffold”
Designs are purified and tested for activity in the lab

Computational enzyme design
If you are interested here are some links:
First reported in 2004: http:// science.sciencemag.org /content/304/5679/1967
Then retracted for lack of reproducibility in 2008
First successful case reported in 2008
https://www.nature.com/nature/journal/v453/n7192/full/nature06879.html [https://www.nature.com/nature/journal/v453/n7192/full/nature06879.html]
For reviews:
Here [http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2001037014600878] And here [http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/anie.201204077/abstract?systemMessage=Wiley+Online+Library+will+be+unavailable+on+Saturday+7th+Oct+from+03.00+EDT+/+08:00+BST+/+12:30+IST+/+15.00+SGT+to+08.00+EDT+/+13.00+BST+/+17:30+IST+/+20.00+SGT+and+Sunday+8th+Oct+from+03.00+EDT+/+08:00+BST+/+12:30+IST+/+15.00+SGT+to+06.00+EDT+/+11.00+BST+/+15:30+IST+/+18.00+SGT+for+essential+maintenance.+Apologies+for+the+inconvenience+caused+.]
Computationally designed enzymes are still very slow, field is still new

Are all enzymes proteins?
Vast majority of chemical reactions in biology are catalyzed by enzymes
Few chemical reactions are carried out by RNA (ribozymes)
Ribozymes - segments of RNA that display enzyme activity
Examples:
RNase P: breaks RNA
Peptidyl transferase : creates peptide bonds during protein synthesis at the ribosome

How does the ribosome function?
mRNA codes for protein sequence
Each 3 nucleotides code for a single amino acid
Amino acids are brought to the ribosome by tRNA
Peptide bond is formed between the incoming a.a . and the growing protein chain
Reaction is catalyzed by rRNA

How does the ribosome function?
The ribosome is a collection of proteins and rRNA associated tightly together to form a large 3D structure
The ribosome is a machine for protein synthesis
The rRNA within the ribosome acts as a catalyst to for peptide bond formation
Atomic structure of one subunit of a ribosome. Blue: proteins, orange: RNA, Green: active site
Cool animation here [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ribosome#/media/File:Protein_translation.gif]

What’s the mechanism
Peptide bond formation is catalyzed by an adenosine (A76), using the ribose sugar hydroxyl groups
C-term of the peptide chain is attached to the tRNA via an ester bond
Proton exchange between the two hydroxyls of the ribose allows amino group of a.a . to attack the carbonyl
Peptide bond is formed

RNA world theory
Hypothesis since the 1980’s that RNA molecules can act as enzymes (ribozymes)
Some examples were well characterized (such as RNA processing)
In 2000: Discovery that peptide bond formation in the ribosome
Reinforced the “RNA world theory”
Early life relied on RNA for catalysis
Result: formation of peptides  proteins  enzymes
Enzymes are much more proficient that ribozymes

Summary
Enzyme inhibitors as drugs:
More potent = low Ki
Km/Ki is a measure of potency
Some enzymes require cofactors
Metals or vitamins
Coupling: catalyze “non-spontaneous” reactions
Enzymes can be designed:
Cat. Antibodies or computational design
Not all enzymes are proteins
RNA (ribozymes)
